retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teeth -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: There are four stages in the development of the tooth germ. [1] [2] These stages include: Bud stage Cap stage Early bell stage Late bell stage

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teeth -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Root formation: Once the crown formation is completed, root formation starts. The Hertwig's root sheath formed by the downward growth of the inner and outer enamel epithelium grows in the apical direction, encloses the dental papilla, and outlines the shape of the root. The dental follicle forming the periodontal ligament and cementum lies external to this sheath.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tooth -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: The process of tooth development begins with the tissues of the first pharyngeal arch. It is a complex process resulting from interactions between the oral cavity ectoderm, which gives rise to cells that produce enamel. The neural crest ectomesenchyme gives rise to tooth structures other than enamel. Primary dentition begins to form in utero between 6 and 8 weeks of gestation. Permanent teeth begin to form in utero at 20 weeks of gestation. If the structures do not start to develop at or near these times, they do not develop at all. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teeth -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Bud stage: This stage starts in the eighth week of intrauterine life with the emergence of enamel organs. The enamel organs are swellings formed by dental lamina under the influence of mesenchymal cells. From these enamel, organs develop each tooth.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Number -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: During normal tooth development, various complications can occur that affect the number of developing teeth. These dental anomalies range from the absence of all teeth (anodontia) to having more teeth than the normal number of teeth (hyperdontia). When describing missing or absent teeth, some may also use the term agenesis, which is the failure of all or part of an organ to develop during embryonic growth. While mild cases of dental agenesis may not even be noticed until later in life, some forms of dental agenesis can be devastating and debilitating, resulting in a poor quality of life in affected individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teeth -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The process of development of teeth is a very complex process resulting from interactions between the ectoderm of the oral cavity, which gives rise to cells that produce enamel, and the neural crest ectomesenchyme which gives rise to the tooth structures other than enamel. At first, i.e., during the six weeks of intrauterine life, the tooth germ starts growing, and the cells forming the mineralized portion start differentiating. Thereafter, dentin and enamel matrices are laid down by these cells, which later start mineralizing. As the completed tooth erupts into the oral cavity, components of the periodontium, which includes periodontal ligament, cementum, and alveolar bone, start surrounding the root.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Primary Dentition -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Teeth are formed from mesenchyme derived from the neural crest and surface ectoderm. The steps involved in the embryogenesis of teeth are complex, with the main components of this multi-step process including [5] [6] [7] : Formation of the dental placode Formation of the dental buds Formation of the dental papilla Formation of the enamel knot Transition to the cap stage in tooth morphogenesis Formation of enamel and dentin Tooth eruption upon the formation of the crown

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teeth -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Periodontal ligament formation: Cells of the dental follicle other than cementoblasts differentiate into fibroblasts that secrete collagen. When the formation of cementum starts, these collagen fibers secreted by fibroblasts within the dental follicle orientate themselves into bundles perpendicular to the surface of the root and form the principal fibers of the periodontal ligament. One end of these fibers become embedded in the developing cementum, and the other end in the alveolar bone and these are known as Sharpey's fibers

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Dental development is a highly regulated biological process and results from several specific and highly regulated sequences of steps. Teeth are comprised of many different internal and external structures with a variety of developmental and embryologic origins. If any of these developmental processes are disturbed or over-exaggerated, changes can occur within the developed tooth’s structure. These changes can be as simple as a slight overexpression of a singular tooth structure to the complete obliteration of dental tissue in final development. The developmental anomalies to be discussed in this article are as follows: dens evaginatus, dens invaginatus, amelogenesis imperfecta, and dentinogenesis imperfecta.

retrieved_docs.title: Developmental Disturbances of the Teeth, Anomalies of Structure -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The morphological changes seen in dens evaginatus are thought to occur early in odontogenesis, during the morphodifferentiation stage of tooth development. [13] It is suggested that proliferation and subsequent evagination of the inner enamel epithelium and the odontogenic mesenchyme into the enamel organ result in the “talon cusp” development. [14] Dens invaginatus results from an alteration of the developing tooth structure during the proliferation and morphodifferentiation stage of tooth development. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teeth -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: As the enamel matures, the crystallites within the enamel increase in size, and the organic content reduces. Once the enamel formation completes, the ameloblast cell loses the Tomes' process, flattens, and becomes the reduced enamel epithelium. This layer protects the enamel during eruption and eventually become the junctional epithelium.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teeth -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Cap stage: This stage is marked by the growth and expansion of the enamel organ, which results in the formation of a concavity in its inner aspect. The growth continues and at about 12 weeks of intrauterine life i.e., during the late cap stage, inner enamel epithelium forms from the inner cuboidal cells of the enamel organ. The inner enamel epithelium cells are columnar in shape, unlike enamel organ cells, which are cuboidal. This layer defines the shape of the crown and later differentiates into ameloblasts responsible for the formation of enamel. The cells from the outer layer of the enamel organ form the outer enamel epithelium and remain cuboidal. They maintain the enamel organ's shape.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teeth -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: At 14 weeks of intrauterine life, apart from the inner and outer enamel epithelium, two other layers, namely stratum intermedium, and stellate reticulum, is formed. The stratum intermedium lies over the inner enamel epithelium and consists of 2 or 3 layers of cells. Its functions include transportation of the nutrients to and from the enamel forming cells, the ameloblasts. The stellate reticulum layer lies between the stratum intermedium and the outer enamel epithelium. The cells in this layer are star-shaped, hence the name stellate. Their function is to protect the underlying dental tissues and maintain the shape of the tooth. Further growth of the inner and the outer enamel epithelium cells forms a cervical loop where the two entities meet and eventually form Hertwig's root sheath, which determines the shape of the root. Bell stage: At this stage, the dental lamina disintegrates and is ready for the formation of dental hard tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Teeth -- Embryology
retrieved_docs.content: Teeth form via the process of odontogenesis. Human dentition is diphyodont, meaning there are two generations of functional teeth during life: deciduous teeth and permanent teeth. Humans have 20 deciduous, or temporary baby, teeth that get replaced by 32 succedaneous, or permanent adult, teeth. Around the sixth- to eight-week of embryonic development, the odontogenesis of deciduous teeth begins. In the twentieth week of embryonic development, the odontogenesis of permanent teeth begins. Every tooth develops from the ectoderm and the ectomesenchyme. The enamel arises from the ectoderm. The dentin, cementum, periodontal ligament, and pulp arise from the ectomesenchyme, which comprises the transformation of neural crest cells into developing maxillary and mandibular arches. [3] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Tooth -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: Two main sets of dentition exist: a primary dentition (colloquially referred to as baby teeth) and an adult or permanent dentition. Under normal physiological conditions, a human has 20 deciduous (baby) teeth and 32 permanent teeth (see Image. Permanent Teeth, Viewed From the Right).

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teeth -- Development
retrieved_docs.content: The condensed mesenchymal cells beneath the inner enamel epithelium form the dental papilla, which later gives forms pulp. The enamel organ has a surrounding fibrous capsule known as the dental follicle, which later forms the periodontal ligament.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2329
retrieved_docs.content: Infancy is identified as a specific developmental stage that belongs to the broader category of the period of life between birth and death, situating this early phase within the overall lifespan continuum.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 5 -- Community 2941
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is associated with the pathophysiology of داء السكري أثناء الحمل (gestational diabetes), اِضطِراب اللسان (tongue disorder), and رضح الولادة (post‑partum lactation). In each of these conditions, the underlying pathophysiological mechanisms are described using the same qualifying concept, indicating a shared thematic element across a metabolic disorder of pregnancy, an oral motor disorder, and the physiological changes of lactation after birth. This common qualifier helps clinicians recognize related mechanistic pathways when evaluating and managing these diverse yet pathophysiologically linked conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 5 -- Community 2903
retrieved_docs.content: Allowed qualifier is linked to several etiological concepts. It is associated with the etiology of ولادة قبل الأوان (premature birth) and also with the etiology of التموت (death). In addition, acquired foot deformities are identified as an etiological factor related to the allowed qualifier.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 5 -- Community 2928
retrieved_docs.content: In embryology, the set of permitted descriptors—referred to as “Allowed qualifier”—includes the entities بيلة سِّيسْتينِية, فالج, and قرحات الطرفين السفليين, indicating that these specific conditions are recognized as relevant qualifiers within the study and classification of embryologic development.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2304
retrieved_docs.content: Early, first or unspecified trimester hemorrhage is classified as spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 161
retrieved_docs.content: scarring into a unified classification scheme that supports recognition, documentation, and management of pregnancy‑related complications across multiple languages and clinical decision‑making pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2303
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium” includes, as a child concept, the specific condition “Uterine size for dates discrepancy.” This specific condition is also classified as a “Finding related to pregnancy,” indicating that uterine size for dates discrepancy is both a maternal condition that can complicate pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium and a particular type of pregnancy‑related clinical finding.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 68
retrieved_docs.content: that complicates pregnancy, childbirth, and the puerperium** is a subtype of **disease of the circulatory system complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium** and is encompassed by **Hypertension AND/OR vomiting complicating pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium**, situating hypertensive disorders of pregnancy within the larger spectrum of maternal circulatory diseases. Additional entities integrated into the framework include **partial molar pregnancy (mola hydatidosa partialis)**, **miscarriage complicated by genital‑pelvic infection** (mapped to **spontaneous abortion**, indicating infection‑related pregnancy loss), and **Face delivery (finding)**, which is linked to the abnormal delivery category “bevalling; abnormaal” and occurs during the maternal pregnancy period, illustrating an abnormal fetal presentation at delivery. The overarching concept **LABOR AND PARTURITION** is parented by **threatened labor** (“مخاض مهدد”), reinforcing that early or threatened labor is

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 80
retrieved_docs.content: and may manifest as a short cervical length during the maternal pregnancy period. Other maternal status conditions include Uterine size for dates discrepancy, identified as a “Finding related to pregnancy” that can be observed throughout gestation, and Spotting complicating pregnancy, unspecified trimester, which is grouped with hemorrhagic events such as early‑trimester bleeding, abruptio placenta, and placenta previa. These hemorrhagic conditions belong to the Standardized MedDRA Query إنهاء الحمل وخطر الإجهاض (termination of pregnancy and risk of miscarriage), itself a member of the category ولادة جنين ميت (birth of a dead fetus). Additional pregnancy‑related entities captured in the framework are partial molar pregnancy (mola hydatidosa partialis), miscarriage complicated by genital‑pelvic infection (which maps to spontaneous abortion and is recorded as a pregnancy outcome), and surgical termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل (جراحي)), also linked to the “termination of pregnancy

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 68
retrieved_docs.content: an abnormal fetal presentation at delivery. The overarching concept **LABOR AND PARTURITION** is parented by **threatened labor** (“مخاض مهدد”), reinforcing that early or threatened labor is identified as threatened labor. Collectively, these interrelated concepts delineate how cervical changes, bleeding, placental disorders, hypertensive and circulatory diseases, molar pregnancies, infectious miscarriages, and abnormal delivery findings are organized within the clinical framework of pregnancy, labor, and postpartum care, supporting diagnosis, risk assessment, and management of pregnant patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2320
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is directly linked to the outcome of a liveborn infant, indicating that reaching this stage of pregnancy can result in a newborn who is alive at birth. In the broader classification of pregnancy timelines, the concept of Duration of gestation encompasses liveborn as a specific category or outcome, reflecting that livebirth is considered one of the possible results within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. This relationship underscores the clinical relevance of gestational age in determining the likelihood of a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 200
retrieved_docs.content: Hypertension is positioned as a circulatory‑system disease that can co‑occur with vomiting and influences both maternal and fetal outcomes. Pregnancy‑related anatomic and clinical findings such as uterine size‑for‑dates discrepancy, spotting in the early, first or unspecified trimester, partial molar pregnancy (mola hydatidosa partialis) and malposition/malpresentation of the fetus are catalogued as child concepts of “Other conditions or status of the mother complicating pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium” or of “NORMAL DELIVERY, AND OTHER INDICATIONS FOR CARE IN PREGNANCY, LABOR, AND DELIVERY.” Miscarriage complicated by genital‑pelvic infection is mapped to spontaneous abortion, highlighting infection‑related pregnancy loss as a type of spontaneous abortion. The maternal pregnancy period serves as the temporal framework that includes both normal obstetric processes and pathological events. Normal pregnancy and delivery are captured under “other pregnancy and delivery including

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 153
retrieved_docs.content: of pregnancy, childbirth and the puerperium,” highlighting its role as a metabolic complication that requires specific management. Additional maternal‑status conditions that complicate pregnancy, childbirth, or the puerperium include uterine size‑for‑dates discrepancy (a pregnancy finding observed during the maternal pregnancy period) and various forms of pregnancy loss such as partial molar pregnancy, miscarriage complicated by genital‑pelvic infection (mapped to spontaneous abortion), regular abortion, and stillbirth. Abnormal delivery findings, exemplified by face delivery, are children of the parent concept “abnormal delivery” and are recorded as occurrences within the maternal pregnancy period. Collectively, the knowledge base interconnects pelvic‑organ abnormalities, cervical pathologies, hemorrhagic and metabolic disorders, and pregnancy‑loss entities within a hierarchical framework of obstetric complications. This structure guides clinicians in recognizing, categorizing, and
